title: LinkedIn Tools
description: Built a public Python monorepo for LinkedIn workflows: networking, recruiter and agency outreach, opportunity research, comment extraction, review, and guarded browser actions.
seoTitle: LinkedIn Tools Case Study | Guarded LinkedIn Automation Controller
seoDescription: A case study on LinkedIn Tools, a Python, SQLite, FastAPI, and Playwright monorepo for guarded LinkedIn networking, recruiter outreach, opportunity intelligence, comment extraction, and review workflows.
constraints: The system had to separate read-only recommendation workflows from browser actions that can send messages, connections, or withdrawals.
The public portfolio artifact could not expose local LinkedIn runtime state, private browser artifacts, credentials, or live account data.
problem: LinkedIn networking, recruiter outreach, opportunity discovery, comment extraction, and acceptance follow-up work were too risky to run as scattered browser scripts.
The hard part was ownership: which system could send, which system could only recommend, what evidence was captured, and what needed reconciliation after uncertain browser behavior.
solution: Built a Python monorepo with one top-level CLI and namespaces for network automation, recruiter/agency outreach, opportunity research, comment extraction, local review UI, and cutover audits.
Put risky actions behind controller-owned states, browser artifacts, audit reports, and explicit flags such as allow-send and allow-withdraw.
Added a local FastAPI/Jinja review UI that reads SQLite and JSON state for opportunities, network runs, recruiter/agency queues, browser artifacts, and guarded actions.
result: Moved LinkedIn automation behind controllers with source contracts, reconciliation reports, candidate reservoirs, acceptance ledgers, review queues, and token-gated action pages.
Separated the boundaries: network owns connection requests and accepted follow-ups, recruiter/agency outreach sends drafted messages only, and opportunity intelligence remains recommend-only.
Created a public example of browser automation where state, evidence, and approval gates are part of the product, not cleanup work after the fact.
deliveryHighlights: One CLI exposes the active network, recruiter-agency, opportunity, comments, and review UI namespaces.
Real sends and withdrawals require explicit flags close to the browser action.
Tests cover source registry contracts, post queues, browser-layer behavior, network reconciliation, recruiter/agency state, storage migrations, and review UI surfaces.
